lexical.title: terraform apply command
lexical.snippet: The terraform apply command executes the operations proposed in a Terraform plan. If you run terraform apply without a saved plan file, Terraform creates a new execution plan, prompts for approval, and then performs the indicated operations. The -auto-approve option skips the approval prompt. If you pass a saved plan f

semantic.title: terraform plan command
semantic.snippet: The terraform plan command creates an execution plan so you can preview changes before Terraform modifies infrastructure. By default, Terraform reads current state for existing remote objects, compares the current configuration with prior state, and proposes the actions needed to make real infrastructure match configur

lexical.title: terraform state commands
lexical.snippet: Terraform uses persisted state data to keep track of the resources it manages. The terraform state commands provide advanced state management without editing state files directly. You can use terraform state subcommands such as list, mv, pull, replace-provider, rm, and show when you need to inspect or safely manipulate

results.question: What is a backend in Terraform?

lexical.title: Backend block configuration overview
lexical.snippet: The backend block defines where Terraform stores its state data files. Terraform can store state locally on disk with the default local backend or in a remote backend so multiple users and automation systems can share state safely. To configure a backend, add a nested backend block inside the top-level terraform block.

semantic.title: Backend block configuration overview
semantic.snippet: The backend block defines where Terraform stores its state data files. Terraform can store state locally on disk with the default local backend or in a remote backend so multiple users and automation systems can share state safely. To configure a backend, add a nested backend block inside the top-level terraform block.

lexical.title: Workspaces
lexical.snippet: A Terraform workspace is a named state instance stored in a backend for one configuration. A backend starts with a default workspace, and some backends support multiple named workspaces so the same configuration can manage separate state for environments such as dev, staging, or production. Terraform CLI workspaces are

lexical.title: Use input variables to add module arguments
lexical.snippet: Input variables define the input interface for a module. When a child module declares variable blocks, a parent module can pass values into that child module by setting arguments in the module block. Module inputs make a module reusable because consumers can provide values such as instance type, subnet, environment, or

semantic.title: Modules overview
semantic.snippet: A Terraform module is a collection of resources that Terraform manages together. Every configuration has a root module in its working directory, and module blocks call child modules from that root module. Child modules can be reused multiple times and modules can call nested child modules. Modules help codify repeated

lexical.snippet: List Resource: aws_instance ~> Note: The aws_instance List Resource is in beta. Its interface and behavior may change as the feature evolves, and breaking changes are possible.
